introduction.title: Aurora Home Documentation
introduction.description: Aurora Home is a complete home automation platform combining an ESP32 firmware and a Next.js web application to monitor 5 environmental sensors in real time.
introduction.systemDescription: The system relies on two independent components: an ESP32 microcontroller that collects environmental sensor data and publishes it via MQTT, and a Next.js web application that consumes this data, stores it in a SQLite database and displays it on a real-time dashboard with Server-Sent Events updates.
introduction.esp32CardDesc: ESP32 microcontroller with 3 I2C sensors: SCD30 (CO₂, temperature, humidity), BME280 (temperature, humidity, pressure) and BH1750 (brightness). Operates in autonomous WiFi access point mode.
introduction.mqttCardDesc: The ESP32 creates its own WiFi network (AP mode) and publishes data every ~10 seconds on the topic
introduction.mqttCardDesc2: via a Mosquitto broker on Orange Pi 3 LTS.
introduction.dashboardCardDesc: Next.js application with instant updates via Server-Sent Events, Recharts graphs for history, SQLite storage via Prisma and PWA support for mobile installation.
introduction.authCardDesc: Passwordless authentication system via Better Auth: the user receives a 6-digit OTP code by email (or on I2C display in hardware mode).
introduction.ctaDesc: Follow the installation guide to get started in minutes.

installationEsp32.sw3Desc: CP2102 or CH340 depending on your board
installationEsp32.wiringDesc: The 3 sensors use the I2C bus on pins
installationEsp32.wiringDesc3: . All sensors share the same SDA/SCL wires.
installationEsp32.step1Desc: Clone the firmware project from GitHub:
installationEsp32.apModeDesc3: (it is not a client of a router). The Orange Pi must connect to this "ESP32-AP-Broker" network to access the MQTT broker.
installationEsp32.step4Desc: Connect the ESP32 via USB and flash the firmware:
installationEsp32.step5Desc: Open the serial monitor to verify startup:
installationEsp32.depsDesc: Libraries are automatically installed by PlatformIO from
installationEsp32.verificationDesc: If you see "Publishing MQTT" in the serial monitor, the firmware is working correctly. Now connect the Orange Pi to the "ESP32-AP-Broker" network and start the Aurora Home web application.

dashboard.title: Dashboard
dashboard.description: This is the main screen of Aurora Home. It displays in real time the measurements of your environment: temperature, humidity, pressure, CO₂ and brightness.
dashboard.realtimeCardDesc: You don't need to do anything — the displayed values refresh automatically as soon as the sensor sends a new measurement. Approximately every 10 seconds, the numbers update with a smooth animation.
dashboard.realtimeReconnect: If your connection is lost, the dashboard reconnects automatically after a few seconds.
dashboard.sensorsIntro: The dashboard displays five cards, one per data type. Here is what each measurement represents and in what unit it is expressed.
dashboard.tempDesc: The ambient temperature of the room, measured by two sensors whose values are averaged for greater accuracy.
dashboard.tempNormal: Between 18°C and 26°C for a comfortable interior.
dashboard.humDesc: The relative humidity of the air, averaged between two sensors.
dashboard.humNormal: Between 40% and 60% is ideal for health and comfort.
dashboard.pressDesc: The ambient air pressure. Allows tracking weather variations.
dashboard.co2Desc: The concentration of carbon dioxide in the air. Indicates air quality and room ventilation.
dashboard.co2Normal: Below 800 ppm: healthy air. Above 1500 ppm: ventilate the room.
dashboard.lightDesc: The ambient light level measured by the BH1750 sensor.
dashboard.lightNormal: 0 lx: complete darkness. 500 lx: good desk lighting.
dashboard.clickCardTitle: Click a card to see the chart
dashboard.clickCardDesc: Each card displays a mini chart. Click on it to enlarge it and see the history of the last 20 measurements with the date, time and exact value for each point.
dashboard.noDataDesc: If the ESP32 sensor is not on or has not yet sent a measurement, the card displays
dashboard.noDataDesc2: (or the corresponding unit) and the chart is empty. This is normal — data will appear as soon as the sensor is active.
dashboard.bannerIntro: Above the sensor cards, a banner permanently indicates the status of your home.
dashboard.bannerHealthyDesc: All sensors are within normal ranges — no anomaly detected.
dashboard.bannerAlertDesc: A colored banner per anomaly (red / orange / yellow). Multiple alerts stack if multiple sensors are anomalous simultaneously. Each banner can be closed individually.
dashboard.bannerWatchingDesc: You closed the banners but anomalies remain unresolved. The green banner only returns when values return to normal ranges or you mark alerts as resolved from the notification panel.
dashboard.pwaTitle: Install the app on your phone
dashboard.pwaDesc: Aurora Home can be installed as a native application on your smartphone. On
dashboard.pwaDescMid: , open the site in Chrome and tap "Add to home screen". On
dashboard.pwaDescEnd: , use the Share button in Safari, then "On home screen". The app then opens like any other app, without the browser navigation bar.
dashboard.iaqIntro: An IAQ score card is displayed at the top of the dashboard, above the sensor grid. It summarizes overall indoor air quality in a single number (0–100).
dashboard.iaqScoreCardDesc: The score is calculated from three weighted sensors: temperature (33 pts), humidity (34 pts), CO₂ (33 pts). Each component receives the maximum points if the value is within the ideal range, then decreases proportionally toward 0 outside it.
dashboard.iaqExcellentDesc: All three parameters are within their optimal ranges.
dashboard.iaqModerateDesc: One or more parameters deviate from the ideal range.
dashboard.iaqPoorDesc: Insufficient conditions — ventilation or correction recommended.
dashboard.trendIntro: Each sensor card shows a small trend arrow indicating whether the value is rising, falling or stable.
dashboard.trendCardDesc: The trend is calculated by comparing the latest value to the average of the previous 3 readings. An upward arrow indicates a rise, a downward arrow a fall, a horizontal arrow indicates stability.
dashboard.chartPeriodIntro: In the chart drawer (opened by clicking a card), buttons let you choose the displayed time window.
dashboard.chartPeriodDownsample: The 24h and 7d views are downsampled to a maximum of 80 points using bucket averaging to stay smooth.
dashboard.chartPeriodXAxis: The X-axis label adapts: DD/MM for 7d, DD/MM HH:mm for 6h-24h, HH:mm for Live/1h.
dashboard.csvExportDesc: An "Export CSV" button is available in the chart drawer, next to the period filters. It downloads the currently displayed data as a CSV file.
dashboard.csvExportFormat: File name: '{type}'_'{period}'_'{timestamp}'.csv — Columns: date, value, type.

alerts.title: Alerts & Anomalies
alerts.description: Aurora Home continuously analyzes your sensor data and alerts you as soon as a value goes out of range or evolves abnormally.
alerts.bannerIntro: Above the sensor cards, a banner permanently indicates the status of your home. It has three possible states:
alerts.healthyDesc: All sensors are within normal ranges — no active unresolved alert. The banner is green with the house icon.
alerts.activeAlertsDesc: A colored banner per anomalous sensor. Each alert displays: the message, the severity badge and the first suggestion. You can close each banner individually with the ×.
alerts.watchingDesc: You have closed all alert banners but the anomalies have not yet been resolved. The system is still monitoring — the green banner only appears when sensors actually return to normal ranges.
alerts.bellDesc: The bell in the top right displays a colored badge when an unread alert exists. The color reflects the highest severity: red (Urgent), orange (Problem), yellow (Warning).
alerts.bellCta: Click the bell to open the
alerts.tabAlertsDesc: All active alerts (unresolved), sorted by date.
alerts.tabUnseenDesc: Active alerts you haven't read yet.
alerts.tabResolvedDesc: History of alerts marked as resolved.
alerts.markSeenDesc: Click on an alert to mark it as read. The white dot in the top right disappears and the bell badge decrements. You can also click
alerts.markSeenDesc2: to handle all alerts at once.
alerts.markResolvedDesc2: at the bottom of the card to archive the alert. It moves to the "Resolved" tab. The dashboard banner updates instantly — if it was the last anomaly, the green "Your home is in good health" banner reappears.
alerts.warningDesc: Value slightly out of range. Not urgent but worth monitoring.
alerts.highDesc: Value significantly out of range. Intervention recommended.
alerts.thresholdsIntro: Thresholds used by the anomaly detector for each sensor:
alerts.suddenChangeDesc: In addition to fixed thresholds, the system detects sudden variations — if a value deviates more than 25% from the average of the last 5 measurements, an alert is generated even if the absolute thresholds are not reached. Brightness is not subject to this check.
alerts.antispamDesc: To avoid a flood of notifications, the system does not send a new alert for the same sensor and the same type of problem if a similar unresolved alert already exists in the
alerts.settingsIntro: A Settings page (accessible via the gear icon in the header) allows you to fully customize alert behavior.
alerts.thresholdConfigDesc: For each sensor, you can set custom high/low values and the associated severity (WARNING / HIGH / CRITICAL). These custom values override the default thresholds in the anomaly detector. A 60-second in-memory cache avoids repeated database queries.
alerts.notifPrefsDesc: From the Settings page, you can:
alerts.notifPrefSeverity: Set a minimum severity per sensor (e.g. only receive HIGH and CRITICAL alerts)
alerts.notifPrefQuiet: Configure global quiet hours (e.g. 11pm → 7am) during which no alerts are created. Supports overnight ranges spanning midnight.
alerts.i18nDesc: The entire interface is available in French and English. Alert messages and suggestions are generated client-side from structured data, not stored as French strings in the database. Date formatting uses toLocaleDateString(locale, ...) with locale-aware output (no comma, capitalized).

auth.title: Log in to Aurora Home
auth.description: Aurora Home does not use a password. Instead, a 6-digit code appears on the device screen at each login — simpler and more secure.
auth.step1Desc: On the login page, type the email address you use for Aurora Home. If it's your very first time, you can also provide your first name to personalize your account.
auth.step2Desc: A 6-digit code appears on the Aurora Home device screen. This code is only valid for a few minutes, so be quick!
auth.step3Desc: Return to Aurora Home and enter the 6-digit code shown on the device. If the code is correct, you are logged in.
auth.step4Desc: You go directly to the dashboard. Your session remains active — you won't need to log in again on every visit.
auth.stayLoggedDesc: Once logged in, your session is remembered. You can close the tab and come back later — you will still be logged in, without needing to re-enter a code.
auth.stayLoggedExpiry: The session expires automatically after a long period of inactivity. You will then be redirected to the login page.
auth.logoutDesc: To log out, click on your avatar in the top right of the dashboard to open the profile panel. You will find the
auth.logoutDesc2: button at the top of the panel. Your session is immediately invalidated and you are redirected to the login page.
auth.faq1Q: No code appears on the device, what should I do?
auth.faq1A: Check that the Aurora Home device is on and that its screen is working. Then return to the login page and try again — each attempt generates a new code.
auth.faq2A: The code is only valid for a few minutes. If it has expired, return to the login page to generate a new one that will appear on the device. Also check that you are entering the currently displayed code.
auth.faq3Q: Do I need to create an account?
auth.faq3A: No. The first time you enter your email, the account is created automatically. You can optionally provide your first name to personalize your profile.
auth.faq4A: Yes. There is no password stored — so there is nothing to steal. Only a temporary one-time code is used at each login.

onboarding.title: Onboarding
onboarding.description: On first login, Aurora Home guides you through a three-step setup process to personalize your account.
onboarding.triggerDesc: Onboarding appears automatically after your first OTP authentication, before accessing the dashboard. Once completed, the
onboarding.triggerDesc3: in the database and you will no longer be redirected to this page.
onboarding.step1Desc: Enter the name that will be displayed on the dashboard in the welcome message and on your profile page. You can change it at any time from the profile.
onboarding.step2Desc: Select an avatar style from the
onboarding.step2Desc2: available. Each style generates a unique avatar from your username. The chosen avatar will be displayed in the dashboard header and on your profile.
onboarding.step3Desc2: . The preference is saved in a cookie and can be changed from the profile at any time.
onboarding.validationDesc: Once the 3 steps are completed and the "Finish" button is clicked, your preferences are saved and you are automatically redirected to the dashboard. The session guard checks
onboarding.validationDesc2: on each visit — if this field is
onboarding.validationDesc3: , you will be redirected to onboarding.
onboarding.changeAvatarDesc: Open your profile (avatar at the top left), hover over the avatar to see the edit icon, click to display the selection grid.
onboarding.changeNameDesc: Open your profile, click the pencil icon next to your name, edit it and save.
onboarding.changeLangDesc: Open your profile, click the language selector in the settings. The change applies immediately.

profile.title: Your profile
profile.description: From your profile, you can customize your avatar, edit your name and change the interface language.
profile.accessDesc: On the dashboard, your avatar is displayed in the top right of the screen. Click on it to open a side panel with all your information and personalization options.
profile.avatarDesc: Hover over your current avatar with the mouse (or press it on mobile) to see the edit icon appear. Click on it to switch the card to selection mode — a grid of
profile.avatarDesc2: automatically generated from your username is displayed. Click on the one you like to select it, then close selection mode.
profile.nameDesc: Your name is displayed in the profile. Click the edit icon next to your name to switch to edit mode, type the new name then click outside the field to confirm.
profile.emailDesc: Your email is displayed for information. It is the address where you receive login codes. It cannot be changed from the interface.
profile.langDesc: Click the edit icon next to the current language to display a dropdown menu. Select
profile.langDesc2: . The change applies immediately to the entire interface.
profile.saveCardTitle: "Save" button visible if you made changes
profile.saveDesc: As soon as you modify your name, avatar or language, a
profile.saveDesc2: button appears at the bottom of the panel. Click it to save. If you close the panel without saving, your changes are lost.
profile.langAutoSaveDesc: Exception: when you change the language via the dropdown, the change is applied and saved immediately — no need to click "Save".

architecture.title: System architecture
architecture.description: Overview of communication between the ESP32, the MQTT broker and the Next.js web application.
architecture.esp32Li1: Creates a WiFi network in Access Point mode (SSID: "ESP32-AP-Broker")
architecture.esp32Li2: Reads I2C sensors: SCD30 (CO₂/temp/hum), BME280 (temp/hum/pres), BH1750 (light)
architecture.esp32Li3: Merges temperature and humidity (average SCD30 + BME280) for greater accuracy
architecture.esp32Li4: Publishes a JSON payload on topic
architecture.esp32Li5: Scans IPs 192.168.4.2–5 to find the MQTT broker automatically
architecture.serverLi1: starts the MQTT client at Next.js server boot
architecture.serverLi2: parses messages, extracts numeric values, saves to DB
architecture.serverLi4: streams updates to connected SSE clients
architecture.serverLi5: Better Auth authentication, session and user profile management
architecture.frontendLi1b: opens an SSE connection and maintains React state
architecture.frontendLi2: Initial data is loaded server-side via
architecture.frontendLi5: PWA application installable via next-pwa + service worker
architecture.mqttPayloadDesc2: a JSON message with raw values including their units:
architecture.mqttPayloadNote: Note: values are strings with units. The Next.js server extracts the numeric part before storing in the database.
architecture.decision1Desc: The ESP32 creates its own WiFi network rather than connecting to the home WiFi. This simplifies initial configuration: no WiFi credentials to enter, the Orange Pi connects directly to the ESP32.
architecture.decision2Desc: Temperature and humidity are measured by two sensors (SCD30 and BME280) then averaged. This reduces individual measurement error and provides redundancy in case of sensor failure.
architecture.decision3Desc: Next.js 14+ offers instrumentation.ts as an entry point for server-side startup effects. It is the ideal place to initialize the MQTT connection without needing a separate Node.js server.
architecture.decision4Desc: Instead of WebSockets, the project uses Server-Sent Events (one-directional server → client). The internal EventEmitter broker decouples MQTT reception from SSE broadcasting, allowing multiple simultaneous clients.

api.title: REST API
api.description: The API routes exposed by the Aurora Home Next.js application.
api.infoNote: The API is designed for internal use. It is not public and does not require explicit authentication for the SSE stream (accessible from the connected dashboard only). User auth goes through Better Auth cookies.
api.sseDesc: Real-time Server-Sent Events (SSE) connection. The server sends an event on each new MQTT message received, as well as a keepalive ping every 15 seconds to keep the connection open.
api.authDesc: Catch-all route managed by Better Auth. Handles all authentication operations: session creation, OTP verification, session retrieval, logout.
api.authEndpoint4Desc: Invalidates the session and deletes the cookie
api.alertStreamNote: The SSE stream also sends alert events:
api.localeDesc: Updates the user's language preference. The value is saved in a
api.localeDesc2: cookie and used by next-intl to load FR or EN translations.
api.devEndpointDesc: Development endpoint to simulate sensor data without a physical ESP32. Returns
api.devEndpointDesc3: . Creates DataPoints, launches anomaly detection and emits SSE events.
api.routeHandlersDesc: All API routes use the Next.js App Router route handlers system (files
api.routeHandlersDesc3: folder). They run server-side and have access to all Node.js resources.
api.datapointsEndpointDesc: Returns historical data for a sensor over a given period. The type parameter is a DataType (e.g. CO2). The period parameter accepts: live, 1h, 6h, 24h, 7d. The 24h and 7d views are downsampled to a maximum of 80 points.
api.thresholdsGetDesc: Returns all custom thresholds stored in the database (SystemThreshold model). Sensors without a custom threshold use default values.
api.thresholdsPostDesc: Creates or updates (upsert) a threshold for a given sensor.
api.preferencesGetDesc: Returns per-sensor preferences (SensorPreference) and global notification settings (NotificationSettings).
api.preferencesPostDesc: Creates or updates a preference. The type field determines whether a sensor preference (sensor) or global settings (settings) is updated.

database.title: Database
database.description: Aurora Home uses Prisma ORM with SQLite. The schema is defined in
database.description2: at the root of the project.
database.sessionDesc: Authenticated session — managed by Better Auth.
database.accountDesc: Account linked to an OAuth provider (Better Auth).
database.verificationDesc: OTP verification token — generated on each login request.
database.dataPointDesc: Sensor reading — created on each MQTT message received.
database.dataPointTypeNote: Enum: TEMPERATURE | HUMIDITY | PRESSURE | CO2 | LIGHT
database.alertValueNote: Value measured at the time of the alert
database.enumDesc2: field of DataPoint is stored as
database.enumDesc4: not Float. The server extracts the numeric value with
database.enumDesc5: before calculating or displaying charts. The
database.enumDesc6: field of Alert is a JSON stringified array.
database.prismaCmd1Desc: Applies the schema without creating a migration
database.prismaCmd2Desc: Creates and applies a versioned migration
database.prismaCmd3Desc: Graphical interface to explore the DB
database.prismaCmd4Desc: Populates the DB with test data (Faker.js)
database.repositoryDesc: All database accesses go through dedicated repositories that encapsulate Prisma queries. Usecases return a
database.systemThresholdDesc: Custom threshold per sensor — overrides the anomaly detector's default values.
database.sensorPreferenceDesc: Alert preference per sensor — enabled state and minimum severity.
database.notificationSettingsDesc: Global notification settings — quiet hours range.
database.systemThresholdHighSeverityNote: Severity associated with the high threshold
database.systemThresholdLowSeverityNote: Severity associated with the low threshold
database.sensorPrefEnabledNote: true by default — false disables alerts for this sensor
database.sensorPrefMinSeverityNote: Minimum severity to trigger an alert (WARNING by default)
database.notifSettingsIdNote: Fixed id "default" — a single global record

mqtt.title: MQTT & Sensors
mqtt.description: Real-time communication between the ESP32 firmware and the Next.js application via the MQTT protocol.
mqtt.brokerNote: The broker IP is configurable via the environment variable
mqtt.brokerNote3: . The ESP32 automatically scans IPs 192.168.4.2 to 192.168.4.5 to find the broker.
mqtt.payloadDesc: The ESP32 publishes a JSON object at each successful reading cycle (~every 10 seconds):
mqtt.payloadNote: Values are strings including the unit. The Next.js server uses a regex to extract the numeric part before storage in the database.
mqtt.scd30Notes: NDIR sensor for CO₂. Fixed address. Main trigger of the publication cycle — publishes only when SCD30 signals 'data ready'.
mqtt.bme280Notes: Auto-detected at 0x76, then 0x77 as fallback. NORMAL mode with oversampling. Temperature and humidity fused with SCD30.
mqtt.bh1750Notes: CONTINUOUS_HIGH_RES_MODE mode. ADDR pin connected to GND for address 0x23. Non-blocking — reads only if measurementReady().
mqtt.fusionDesc: Temperature and humidity are measured by two different sensors. The firmware calculates the average to reduce individual measurement errors.
mqtt.lc5Desc: EventEmitter notifies all active SSE streams
mqtt.reconnectDesc: The Next.js MQTT client automatically reconnects on disconnection (
mqtt.reconnectDesc3: function re-scans AP network IPs on broker connection loss.

simulator.title: Sensor simulator
simulator.description: A Node.js script to test the alerts system and real-time updates without physical ESP32 sensors.
simulator.endpointDesc: The simulator posts to this endpoint at each interval. For each sensor provided, the server creates a DataPoint, launches anomaly detection and emits SSE events.
simulator.step1Desc: Creates a DataPoint in the database for each sensor
simulator.step2Desc: Retrieves the last 6 readings to calculate the rolling average
simulator.step3Desc: Calls detectAnomaly() — checks thresholds and sudden variation
simulator.step4Desc: If anomaly detected and no recent alert → creates an Alert + emits alert_created via SSE
simulator.step5Desc: Emits sensor_update via SSE to update dashboard cards
simulator.alertTestDesc: Anomaly modes gradually raise values over ~20 steps until they exceed WARNING then HIGH thresholds. Example with
simulator.alertStep4: → Step ~4: CO₂ exceeds 800 ppm → WARNING alert generated
simulator.alertStep11: → Step ~11: CO₂ exceeds 1500 ppm → HIGH alert generated
simulator.alertStep16: → Step ~16: CO₂ exceeds 2000 ppm → CRITICAL alert generated
simulator.seedCommandsIntro: npm scripts allow you to populate the database with realistic historical data to test charts and the IAQ score.
simulator.cmdSeed7daysDesc: Generates 7 days of realistic data (1 point every 5 min)
simulator.cmdSeed7daysClearDesc: Clears existing data then generates 7 days of data

codeApp.title: Conventions — Next.js Application
codeApp.description: Code and organization standards adopted in the
codeApp.next15Desc: App Router, Server Components, Route Handlers, instrumentation.ts
codeApp.ts5Desc: Strict mode enabled — all explicit types
codeApp.usecaseDesc: All business logic is encapsulated in usecases. The wrapper
codeApp.usecaseDesc3: automatically wraps the function in a try/catch and returns a uniform type
codeApp.usecaseNote2: ). They never throw an exception — the error is always captured in
codeApp.repositoryDesc: All Prisma accesses go through repositories. No direct calls to
codeApp.clientLi2: Components with React hooks, event listeners, SSE, animations
codeApp.datapointUtilsDesc: The ESP32 sends raw values with units (e.g.:
codeApp.datapointUtilsDesc2: ). These functions bridge the database and charts.
codeApp.otpDisplayDesc: Two OTP code display modes controlled by environment variables:
codeApp.otpDevModeDesc: Displays the OTP code in the Next.js console (ASCII art). No hardware required — for local development.
codeApp.otpHardwareDesc: Sends the code to the physical I2C screen via a Node.js script (Linux only, Orange Pi).
codeApp.tsConfigDesc2: mode is enabled. Here are the effective rules that follow and other important options in
codeApp.strictNullChecksDesc: null and undefined are not assignable to other types. You must explicitly handle null cases.
codeApp.noImplicitAnyDesc: Forbids implicit any types. Every function parameter must have an explicit type.
codeApp.strictFunctionTypesDesc: Checks function parameter contravariance — avoids type errors in callbacks.
codeApp.strictPropertyInitDesc: Class properties must be initialized in the constructor or declared with !.
codeApp.noImplicitThisDesc: Forbids use of this with implicit any type.
codeApp.alwaysStrictDesc: Injects "use strict" into each compiled file.
codeApp.tsStyleRulesTitle: TypeScript style rules applied in the project
codeApp.importTypeDesc: Use import type for type-only imports. Reduces bundle size and clarifies dependencies.
codeApp.noAnyDesc: The any type is banned. Use unknown then narrowing, or a precise type.
codeApp.implicitReturnDesc: Simple functions can let TypeScript infer the return type. Usecases and repositories have explicit return types.
codeApp.aliasDesc: All internal imports use the @/ alias (project root). No relative paths ../../../.
codeApp.isolatedModulesDesc: Each .ts/.tsx file must have at least one import or export to be an ES module. Required for Babel/SWC compatibility.
codeApp.testsDesc: for linting and formatting (replaces ESLint + Prettier).
codeApp.useTrendDesc: Calculates the trend direction for a given sensor type by comparing the latest value to the average of the previous 3 readings. Returns "up", "down" or "stable" according to stability thresholds defined per type.
codeApp.settingsPageDesc: Settings page (/settings) accessible via the gear icon in the header. Allows customizing alert thresholds and notification preferences per sensor as well as global quiet hours.
codeApp.iaqModuleDesc: IAQ score calculation module in features/datapoint. Computes a composite 0–100 score from temperature, humidity and CO₂.
codeApp.csvExportDesc: CSV export feature in the chart drawer. Generates a downloadable file with the currently displayed data.
codeApp.i18nClientSideDesc: Alert messages and suggestions generated client-side from structured data, with date formatting via toLocaleDateString(locale).

codeEsp32.description: C++ code standards used in the
codeEsp32.mainCppDesc: The firmware is organized in sections delimited by commented separators:
codeEsp32.patternDesc: Each sensor has two dedicated functions returning a
codeEsp32.criticalErrorDesc: If a required sensor (SCD30, BME280) fails initialization, indefinite blocking:
codeEsp32.nonCriticalErrorDesc: Read errors in the loop are logged but do not interrupt the cycle:

contribution.title: Contribute to the project
contribution.description: Guide for contributing to Aurora Home — whether on the Next.js web application or the ESP32 firmware.
contribution.twoReposDesc: The Aurora Home project consists of two separate Git repositories:

contribution.step2Desc: Create a branch with a descriptive name from

contribution.step5Desc: Push your branch and open a PR on GitHub:

marketing.title: Marketing Site
marketing.description: The Aurora Home storefront and e-commerce site — a Vue 3 SPA showcasing the Aurora One product and handling orders.
marketing.noteDescStripe: Payments are handled via Stripe. The site communicates with a backend on port 3000 to create payment intents.
